attributes.stem:
A 60-year-old woman presents to her General Practitioner with a seven day history of dull lower back pain. This has worsened in the last two days and is now affecting her sleep. The pain is constant, and nothing including positioning seems to make it better or worse. It does not radiate down her legs. The pain not been responsive to regular paracetamol. She recalls no recent trauma.

Her bladder and bowel function are intact. She has been able to mobilise without difficulty and denies any sensory disturbances.

Her medical history includes rheumatoid arthritis, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, and hypertension. Her regular long-term medications include prednisolone, tiotropium, fluticasone/eformoterol, and perindopril/indapamide. Her last menstrual period was 7 years ago.

On examination, she has localised tenderness over the mid-line in the mid-lumbar spine. She has no focal neurological signs in the lower limb.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely cause of her back pain?
attributes.explanation:
This patient most likely has a fragility fracture, given her localised pain with no radiation, no recent trauma, and risk factors for osteoporosis; including that she is post-menopausal, her rheumatoid arthritis, and her corticosteroid use.

Disc herniation is possible; however, she has no radiculopathy or signs of nerve impingement, and no recent trauma; thus, it is less likely than a fragility fracture.

Spinal canal stenosis usually presents with neurological claudication or Cauda-Equina syndrome; neither of which are evident in this patient.

Muscular strain is unlikely to cause tenderness over the bone in the mid-line of the mid-lumbar spine. The pain is also more likely to be cramping in nature, and worse on movement or use.

Spondylolisthesis is a condition characterised by movement of the vertebrae slipping forward onto the vertebral bone below it. It presents with back pain, and often also involves radiculopathy. It is often relieved by lying supine.
learningPoints: The possibility of a fragility fracture must always be considered as a cause for back pain in an older patient on corticosteroids.
references: Curtis EM, Moon RJ, Harvey NC, Cooper C. The impact of fragility fracture and approaches to osteoporosis risk assessment worldwide. Bone. 2017 Nov;104:29-38. doi: 10.1016/j.bone.2017.01.024. Epub 2017 Jan 22. PMID: 28119181; PMCID: PMC5420448.
Rebolledo BJ, Unnanuntana A, Lane JM. A comprehensive approach to fragility fractures. J Orthop Trauma. 2011 Sep;25(9):566-73. doi: 10.1097/BOT.0b013e3181f9b389. PMID: 21654529.
